clinicalStem: A 58-year-old male smoker presents with a 4-month history of persistent hoarseness. Laryngoscopy reveals a small, well-defined lesion confined to the left true vocal cord with normal cord mobility. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma. CT neck shows no regional lymph node involvement. He is staged as T1N0M0 glottic carcinoma. His oncologist counsels him about prognosis. Which of the following best describes the expected 5-year survival for this stage?
options: Approximately 90–95%
Approximately 70–75%
Approximately 50–55%
Approximately 30–35%
Approximately 15–20%
explanation: T1N0M0 glottic carcinoma carries an excellent 5-year survival of approximately 90–95% because the glottis has a sparse lymphatic supply, limiting early nodal spread, and early-stage disease responds well to radiotherapy or endoscopic resection. The rich blood supply and limited lymphatics are why glottic tumors present early with hoarseness before nodal spread. This contrasts sharply with supraglottic tumors, which have abundant lymphatics and poorer early-stage prognosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — T1N0M0 glottic carcinoma has a 90–95% 5-year survival, the best among laryngeal cancers.
70–75% is closer to the survival for T2 glottic or early supraglottic carcinoma, not T1N0 glottic.
50–55% reflects more advanced stages (T3/T4) or supraglottic tumors with nodal involvement.
30–35% is consistent with stage III–IV disease with regional or distant spread.
15–20% applies to metastatic or very advanced laryngeal carcinoma.
clinicalPearl: T1N0M0 glottic SCC has the best prognosis among laryngeal cancers (~90–95% 5-year survival) because the glottis has sparse lymphatics, causing early hoarseness before nodal spread.
keyClue: Confined to true vocal cord, normal cord mobility, T1N0M0 staging
commonExamTrap: Confusing glottic (early hoarseness, excellent prognosis) with supraglottic carcinoma (late presentation, worse prognosis due to rich lymphatics).
missedConcept: The sparse lymphatic drainage of the glottis is the anatomical basis for its excellent prognosis compared to supraglottic tumors.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man with a long-standing tracheostomy for ventilator-dependent respiratory failure develops a sudden massive bleed from the tracheostomy site 3 weeks post-insertion. On examination, the tracheostomy tube is in situ; pulsatile bright red blood is welling up around the tube. His blood pressure is 80/50 mmHg and he is tachycardic at 120 bpm. Which of the following is the most immediately life-saving manoeuvre while awaiting emergency surgical intervention?
options: Over-inflate the tracheostomy cuff and apply digital pressure through the stoma onto the anterior tracheal wall
Remove the tracheostomy tube and pack the stoma with gauze
Administer IV tranexamic acid and order an emergency CT angiogram
Replace the tracheostomy tube with a larger size to tamponade
Apply external neck compression with a firm dressing
explanation: Tracheoinnominate fistula (TIF) is a rare but rapidly fatal complication of tracheostomy, typically occurring 3 days to 6 weeks post-insertion due to pressure necrosis eroding into the innominate artery. The immediate temporising manoeuvre is to over-inflate the tracheostomy cuff to compress the fistula point AND insert a finger through the stoma to compress the innominate artery against the posterior manubrium — this can temporarily control haemorrhage while emergency surgical ligation is arranged. Removing the tube would worsen bleeding and lose the airway simultaneously.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — over-inflation of the cuff and digital anterior compression is the accepted bridge to emergent surgical control of tracheoinnominate fistula.
Removing the tube is catastrophic: it loses the airway and removes the only partial tamponade against the artery.
Tranexamic acid and CT angiogram are too slow; the patient will exsanguinate before imaging is completed.
Upsizing the tube alone will not compress a fistula into the innominate artery.
External neck compression does not reach the innominate artery, which lies deep to the manubrium.
clinicalPearl: Tracheoinnominate fistula presents as sentinel or massive bleed 3 days–6 weeks post-tracheostomy; over-inflate the cuff and apply digital pressure through the stoma as the bridge to emergent surgical ligation.
keyClue: Massive pulsatile bleed 3 weeks post-tracheostomy with haemodynamic instability
commonExamTrap: Reaching for imaging (CT angiogram) before life-saving mechanical tamponade in an actively exsanguinating patient.
missedConcept: Tracheoinnominate fistula is a surgical emergency; cuff over-inflation + digital pressure is the only immediate temporising measure before operative control.
redFlagReason: Tracheoinnominate fistula causes rapid exsanguination; failure to immediately tamponade the bleed is fatal within minutes.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old man with T4a glottic squamous cell carcinoma underwent total laryngectomy and neck dissection 8 months ago. He now presents with a firm, non-tender nodule at the medial edge of his tracheal stoma that has been growing for 6 weeks. He is a current smoker. CT neck demonstrates a 1.8 cm enhancing soft-tissue mass at the stomal margin with no distant metastases. What is the most important prognostic implication of this finding?
options: Stomal recurrence after laryngectomy carries a poor prognosis with median survival under 6 months
Stomal recurrence is usually curable with a single course of radiotherapy alone
This finding most likely represents granulation tissue and urgent re-biopsy is unnecessary
The 5-year survival for stomal recurrence is approximately 50% with salvage surgery
Stomal recurrence indicates systemic metastasis and palliative care should be initiated immediately
explanation: Stomal recurrence (peristomal recurrence) after total laryngectomy is one of the most feared complications and carries a very poor prognosis; median survival is typically less than 6 months even with aggressive treatment. It usually results from subglottic/tracheal tumour involvement, paratracheal node disease, or seeding at the time of surgery. Radiotherapy and re-resection are rarely curative. Early recognition prompts biopsy, staging, and a multidisciplinary decision about palliative vs. limited salvage intent.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — stomal recurrence is associated with a median survival under 6 months and is notoriously difficult to salvage.
Radiotherapy alone rarely achieves durable control of stomal recurrence; outcomes remain poor.
A firm, growing stomal nodule at 8 months post-laryngectomy must be biopsied urgently — assuming granulation tissue without histology is dangerous.
The 5-year survival for stomal recurrence is far lower than 50%; most series report less than 15%.
While prognosis is grave, initiating palliative care without first confirming the diagnosis histologically and completing staging is premature.
clinicalPearl: Stomal recurrence after laryngectomy is a marker of very poor prognosis (median survival <6 months); biopsy and urgent MDT review are mandatory for any new stomal mass.
keyClue: Firm growing nodule at tracheal stoma 8 months after total laryngectomy for T4a glottic SCC
commonExamTrap: Overestimating the salvageability of stomal recurrence; it is not analogous to a local recurrence that can be re-resected with curative intent.
missedConcept: Stomal recurrence signifies tumour that was in or near the subglottis/paratracheal tissue at original surgery and has a distinctly worse prognosis than other post-laryngectomy recurrences.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old male teacher and heavy smoker presents with a 9-month history of hoarseness, throat clearing, and occasional odynophagia. He admits to drinking 30 units of alcohol per week. Laryngoscopy reveals diffuse erythema and oedema of both vocal cords with white patches on the cord surfaces. Biopsy is taken and shows moderate dysplasia (CIN II equivalent). He asks what happens if this is left untreated. Which of the following best describes the risk of malignant transformation for moderate laryngeal dysplasia?
options: Approximately 15–30% of moderate dysplasia lesions progress to invasive carcinoma if untreated
Virtually all moderate dysplasia lesions (>90%) will progress to invasive carcinoma within 2 years
Moderate dysplasia has no significant malignant potential and observation alone is sufficient
Progression from moderate dysplasia to carcinoma in situ takes at least 10–15 years in all cases
Malignant transformation requires HPV co-infection and does not occur from smoking alone
explanation: Moderate laryngeal dysplasia (moderate squamous intraepithelial lesion) carries a malignant transformation risk of approximately 15–30%, with higher-grade (severe) dysplasia and carcinoma in situ carrying risks up to 30–50%. Risk is significantly amplified by ongoing smoking and alcohol use. Close surveillance (laryngoscopy every 3–6 months) and cessation of carcinogens are essential. The white patches described are consistent with leukoplakia, which in the context of dysplasia mandates active management.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — moderate laryngeal dysplasia has a 15–30% malignant transformation risk without treatment, underscoring the need for surveillance and risk-factor modification.
>90% progression is not supported by evidence; this overstates the risk and applies to severe dysplasia/CIS only in some series.
Moderate dysplasia does carry significant malignant potential; observation without active surveillance and smoking cessation is inadequate.
Time to progression is highly variable and can be rapid; a fixed 10–15 year timeline is incorrect and provides false reassurance.
HPV is a risk factor for some head and neck cancers but is not required for dysplastic progression in the larynx; smoking and alcohol are the dominant carcinogens.
clinicalPearl: Moderate laryngeal dysplasia carries ~15–30% malignant transformation risk; smoking cessation, alcohol reduction, and regular laryngoscopic surveillance every 3–6 months are mandatory.
keyClue: White patches on vocal cords, biopsy showing moderate dysplasia, heavy smoker with alcohol use
commonExamTrap: Assuming dysplasia requires HPV co-infection or assuming it always progresses; the variable but significant risk demands surveillance, not reassurance.
missedConcept: Laryngeal leukoplakia with moderate dysplasia is a pre-malignant lesion requiring active follow-up, not dismissal — continued exposure to carcinogens increases progression risk substantially.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe right-sided throat pain, trismus, uvular deviation to the left, and a muffled 'hot potato' voice. She has had fever and dysphagia for 6 days. She was seen 4 days ago and prescribed oral amoxicillin but has worsened. On examination, temperature is 39.4°C, HR 118 bpm, and she appears toxic. She reports new-onset chest pain and shortness of breath. Chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. Which complication has most likely developed?
options: Descending necrotising mediastinitis from a peritonsillar abscess extending into the deep neck spaces
Aspiration pneumonia secondary to dysphagia
Spontaneous pneumothorax from vigorous coughing
Pulmonary embolism from prolonged immobility
Cardiac tamponade from viral pericarditis
explanation: Descending necrotising mediastinitis (DNM) is a rapidly fatal complication of deep space neck infections, most commonly peritonsillar or parapharyngeal abscess. Infection tracks along the fascial planes of the neck into the mediastinum, causing fulminant necrotising infection. The key clue is mediastinal widening on CXR in the setting of an undertreated pharyngeal infection with systemic sepsis and new chest symptoms. Mortality exceeds 40% even with prompt surgical drainage. This patient requires urgent CT neck and chest, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, and urgent surgical consultation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — mediastinal widening + deep neck infection + sepsis = descending necrotising mediastinitis until proven otherwise.
Aspiration pneumonia is possible but would not cause mediastinal widening; typical CXR would show consolidation, not widened mediastinum.
Pneumothorax does not cause mediastinal widening and is not a known complication of peritonsillar abscess.
PE is unlikely in a previously well 32-year-old; it would not cause mediastinal widening in this context.
Viral pericarditis causing tamponade would show an enlarged cardiac silhouette, not mediastinal widening, and is not directly linked to peritonsillar abscess.
clinicalPearl: New chest pain + mediastinal widening in a patient with undertreated peritonsillar/deep neck infection = descending necrotising mediastinitis — a surgical emergency with >40% mortality.
keyClue: Mediastinal widening on CXR with new chest pain in the setting of an untreated peritonsillar abscess
commonExamTrap: Attributing chest symptoms to aspiration pneumonia and missing the rarer but far more dangerous mediastinal spread.
missedConcept: Deep neck infections can track along fascial planes into the posterior mediastinum; the retropharyngeal space communicates directly with the mediastinum.
redFlagReason: Descending necrotising mediastinitis is rapidly fatal (>40% mortality); delayed surgical drainage leads to multi-organ failure and death.

clinicalStem: A 40-year-old woman who required emergency tracheostomy following a road traffic accident is successfully decannulated 3 weeks later. Six weeks post-decannulation she develops progressive exertional dyspnoea and a new biphasic stridor on exertion. She has no fever, no voice change, and examination reveals no laryngeal mass. Flexible nasolaryngoscopy shows subglottic narrowing estimated at 60% of the lumen. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Post-tracheostomy subglottic stenosis
Tracheomalacia at the former cuff site
Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury causing bilateral vocal cord palsy
Exacerbation of pre-existing asthma triggered by the trauma
Tracheo-oesophageal fistula
explanation: Post-tracheostomy subglottic stenosis is a recognised late complication caused by pressure necrosis from the tracheostomy tube or cuff, leading to fibrotic scarring and narrowing of the airway weeks to months after decannulation. Biphasic stridor appearing after decannulation and progressive exertional dyspnoea are the hallmark presentation. Nasolaryngoscopy confirming subglottic narrowing clinches the diagnosis. Management options include endoscopic laser/balloon dilation or open airway reconstruction (cricotracheal resection).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — subglottic stenosis is the classic late complication of tracheostomy presenting with biphasic stridor and progressive dyspnoea after successful decannulation.
Tracheomalacia causes expiratory stridor/collapse and typically occurs at the cuff site rather than the subglottis; it would show dynamic collapse on flexible scope, not fixed narrowing.
Bilateral vocal cord palsy from RLN injury would present as aphonia/weak voice and inspiratory stridor, not primarily exertional biphasic stridor with normal voice.
Asthma produces expiratory wheeze, not fixed biphasic stridor; 40% luminal narrowing on scope rules against pure asthma.
Tracheo-oesophageal fistula presents with coughing on swallowing and aspiration, not biphasic stridor alone.
clinicalPearl: Biphasic stridor with exertional dyspnoea appearing weeks after tracheostomy decannulation = post-tracheostomy subglottic stenosis until proven otherwise.
keyClue: Progressive biphasic stridor 6 weeks after decannulation with subglottic narrowing on nasolaryngoscopy
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing tracheomalacia or bilateral cord palsy when the endoscopic finding is fixed subglottic narrowing (stenosis), not dynamic collapse or cord immobility.
missedConcept: Post-tracheostomy stenosis typically manifests weeks after decannulation once oedema resolves and fibrosis contracts; it does not present during intubation itself.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old male smoker presents with a 3-month history of progressive dysphagia and referred otalgia. Laryngoscopy shows a supraglottic mass involving the epiglottis and left aryepiglottic fold with normal cord mobility. CT neck confirms the primary tumour and reveals bilateral cervical lymph nodes, the largest being 2.8 cm on the right and 1.6 cm on the left, with no extracapsular extension. He is staged as T2N2cM0. Which of the following best describes how bilateral cervical nodal involvement affects his prognosis compared to a similar patient who is N0?
options: Bilateral nodal involvement (N2c) roughly halves the 5-year survival compared to N0 disease
Bilateral nodal involvement has minimal impact on survival if nodes are under 3 cm
N2c disease is associated with a better prognosis than N1 because bilateral nodes indicate a robust immune response
The presence of bilateral nodes means the primary is unresectable and palliation is the only option
5-year survival for N2c supraglottic carcinoma exceeds 80% with chemoradiotherapy
explanation: Bilateral cervical nodal involvement (N2c) in supraglottic carcinoma significantly worsens prognosis. N0 supraglottic carcinoma may achieve 5-year survival of 60–70%, whereas N2 disease reduces this to approximately 30–40%. The abundant lymphatic drainage of the supraglottis means early bilateral spread is common, making nodal status the most important prognostic factor in this subsite. Extracapsular extension would worsen prognosis further. The rough halving of survival is the key prognostic concept.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — N2c disease approximately halves the 5-year survival of supraglottic SCC compared to N0, reflecting the supraglottis's rich bilateral lymphatic network.
Node size below 3 cm does not negate the prognostic impact; bilateral involvement itself confers a stage III–IV classification with significantly worse outcomes.
Bilateral nodes represent tumour spread, not immune response; they universally worsen prognosis.
T2N2cM0 may still be potentially resectable with combined-modality treatment; unresectability depends on T-stage criteria (carotid encasement, prevertebral invasion), not N2c alone.
5-year survival for N2c supraglottic carcinoma is typically 30–45%, not >80%; chemoradiotherapy improves but does not normalise outcomes.
clinicalPearl: Bilateral cervical nodal involvement (N2c) in supraglottic carcinoma roughly halves 5-year survival compared to N0, reflecting the abundant bilateral lymphatic drainage of the supraglottis.
keyClue: Bilateral cervical lymph nodes (N2c) in supraglottic carcinoma
commonExamTrap: Assuming small bilateral nodes have minimal prognostic significance — bilateral involvement itself upstages to N2c and significantly worsens survival.
missedConcept: Nodal status is the single most important prognostic factor in supraglottic (and most head and neck) carcinomas because the supraglottis drains bilaterally through abundant lymphatics.

clinicalStem: A 16-year-old boy presents 3 weeks after an episode of untreated sore throat with fever, a migratory polyarthritis involving his knees and wrists, and a new soft systolic murmur at the apex. Echocardiography confirms mild mitral regurgitation. ASO titre is markedly elevated and throat swab is now negative. He is diagnosed with acute rheumatic fever with carditis. After treatment of the acute episode, what is the most appropriate long-term follow-up recommendation to prevent further episodes?
options: Continuous antibiotic prophylaxis with monthly intramuscular benzathine penicillin G for at least 10 years or until age 21 (whichever is longer)
Treat each future sore throat episode with a 5-day course of amoxicillin on an as-needed basis
Annual echocardiography with no prophylaxis since the acute episode has resolved
Tonsillectomy to prevent further streptococcal colonisation and thereby prevent recurrence
Secondary prophylaxis is not required once the murmur has resolved on echocardiography
explanation: Rheumatic fever with carditis (mitral regurgitation confirmed) requires long-term secondary prophylaxis to prevent recurrent streptococcal pharyngitis and further cardiac damage. WHO and international guidelines recommend continuous benzathine penicillin G 1.2 MU IM every 4 weeks for at least 10 years from the last episode or until age 21 (age 40 if valvular disease is severe or surgery was required). Each recurrence of group A streptococcal infection risks additional valve damage. Symptomatic therapy alone for individual episodes is inadequate prevention.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — monthly benzathine penicillin G is the gold standard for secondary prophylaxis in rheumatic fever with carditis, preventing recurrent streptococcal throat infections.
As-needed treatment does not prevent asymptomatic streptococcal throat carriage or subclinical recurrences that still cause cardiac damage.
Echo surveillance is valuable but without prophylaxis the patient will likely suffer recurrences; surveillance alone is not prophylaxis.
Tonsillectomy does not reliably prevent streptococcal pharyngitis in the rest of the pharynx and is not a substitute for antibiotic prophylaxis.
Resolution of murmur on echo does not eliminate the risk of cardiac damage from future recurrences; prophylaxis duration is determined by carditis severity, not echo normalisation.
clinicalPearl: Rheumatic fever with carditis requires long-term secondary prophylaxis — monthly benzathine penicillin G for ≥10 years or until age 21 (longer if valvular disease persists).
keyClue: Acute rheumatic fever with confirmed mitral regurgitation (carditis) after untreated streptococcal pharyngitis
commonExamTrap: Stopping prophylaxis once the murmur resolves on echocardiography — resolution does not protect against future valve damage from recurrent streptococcal infections.
missedConcept: Secondary prevention of rheumatic fever is continuous, not episodic — each recurrent streptococcal infection risks cumulative valve damage (the 'vicious cycle').

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old unvaccinated man presents to the emergency department with an 8-hour history of rapidly worsening sore throat, high fever (39.8°C), severe odynophagia, drooling, and a muffled voice. He is sitting upright leaning forward and appears anxious. Oropharyngeal examination is limited because he gags excessively. The triage nurse notes inspiratory stridor. His oxygen saturation is 93% on room air. What is the single most critical immediate action?
options: Call anaesthetics and ENT urgently — do not leave the patient, do not attempt examination or instrumentation; prepare for emergency intubation in theatre
Obtain a lateral neck X-ray to confirm 'thumbprint sign' before any intervention
Perform immediate bedside laryngoscopy with a tongue depressor to assess the epiglottis
Administer IM adrenaline nebuliser and send blood cultures before calling for senior help
Start IV ceftriaxone and steroids and observe for 30 minutes for clinical improvement
explanation: Acute epiglottitis in an adult is a life-threatening airway emergency. The classic 'tripod' posture (leaning forward, drooling, muffled voice, stridor, and hypoxia) in an unvaccinated adult with rapid onset indicates impending airway obstruction. The priority is to call senior anaesthetic and ENT help immediately and NOT to perform any examination that could precipitate sudden complete obstruction. The patient must be escorted directly to theatre for controlled intubation (or surgical airway if intubation fails). Lateral neck X-ray (thumbprint sign) is useful but should never delay securing the airway in a deteriorating patient.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — in impending airway obstruction from epiglottitis, the absolute priority is immediate senior call and controlled airway management in a prepared setting.
Lateral X-ray is helpful but delays securing the airway; in a hypoxic, stridulous patient, imaging must never precede airway management.
Bedside laryngoscopy with a tongue depressor can precipitate complete laryngospasm and is absolutely contraindicated in suspected acute epiglottitis.
Adrenaline nebuliser is for croup (laryngotracheobronchitis in children), not epiglottitis; and delaying senior involvement while doing cultures is dangerous.
Starting antibiotics is appropriate but observing for 30 minutes before securing the airway in a patient with saturation 93% and stridor risks fatal obstruction during that window.
clinicalPearl: In acute epiglottitis with stridor and hypoxia, NEVER examine with tongue depressors — call anaesthetics + ENT immediately and take to theatre for controlled intubation.
keyClue: Tripod posture, drooling, muffled voice, inspiratory stridor, and falling O2 saturation = impending total airway obstruction from epiglottitis
commonExamTrap: Performing bedside laryngoscopy or lateral X-ray before calling for senior help — any instrumentation can precipitate fatal complete obstruction.
missedConcept: The tongue depressor examination is absolutely contraindicated in suspected epiglottitis because it can trigger laryngospasm and complete airway obstruction within seconds.
redFlagReason: Acute epiglottitis with falling O2 saturation and stridor = impending fatal airway obstruction; any delay in securing the airway or inadvertent bedside instrumentation can cause immediate death.

clinicalStem: A 60-year-old man successfully treated for T2N0M0 glottic squamous cell carcinoma with definitive radiotherapy 2 years ago attends his follow-up clinic. He continues to smoke 10 cigarettes per day. He denies any new symptoms. His laryngoscopy today shows no local recurrence. In counselling him about long-term prognosis, which of the following is the most clinically significant risk he faces over the next 5–10 years?
options: Development of a second primary malignancy in the head-and-neck, lung, or oesophagus (field cancerisation)
Late radiation-induced hypothyroidism requiring lifelong levothyroxine
Complete loss of voice due to progressive radiation fibrosis
Development of radiation-induced osteonecrosis of the cervical spine
Local recurrence of the original glottic tumour after 2 years of remission is highly unlikely and not a significant concern
explanation: Field cancerisation refers to the widespread carcinogenic effect of tobacco and alcohol across the entire aerodigestive tract epithelium, dramatically increasing the risk of synchronous or metachronous second primary malignancies — most commonly in the lung, oesophagus, oral cavity, or hypopharynx. In survivors of laryngeal SCC who continue smoking, the annual risk of a second primary is approximately 3–7% per year. This means that over 10 years, roughly 20–40% will develop another tobacco-related cancer. Smoking cessation is the single most important modifiable factor. Second primaries are the leading cause of cancer death in long-term laryngeal cancer survivors.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — field cancerisation in a continuing smoker creates a 3–7% annual risk of second primary malignancy in the head-and-neck, lung, or oesophagus; this is the dominant long-term risk.
Radiation-induced hypothyroidism is a real complication of neck irradiation and should be screened for, but it is manageable and not the primary cause of mortality in this group.
Progressive radiation fibrosis causing complete voice loss is uncommon at T2 doses and is far less clinically significant than second primary malignancy.
Radiation osteonecrosis of the cervical spine is extremely rare in standard laryngeal radiotherapy fields.
Late local recurrence (>2 years) is less common but not negligible; however, the continuing smoking habit means second primary risk is the dominant long-term threat.
clinicalPearl: In laryngeal cancer survivors who continue smoking, field cancerisation creates a 3–7% annual risk of second primary malignancy (lung, oesophagus, oral cavity) — the leading long-term survival threat.
keyClue: Ongoing smoking after curative radiotherapy for laryngeal SCC — field cancerisation risk
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on local recurrence surveillance while neglecting the high risk of second primary malignancy in the same carcinogen-exposed field.
missedConcept: Field cancerisation means the entire upper aerodigestive tract has been exposed to carcinogens; treating one tumour does not protect the remaining field from developing new primaries.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man with a tracheostomy inserted 6 weeks ago for respiratory failure secondary to Guillain-Barré syndrome is being assessed for decannulation. He can tolerate cuff deflation for 48 hours with adequate oxygenation and is able to phonate. Flexible laryngoscopy confirms patent supraglottic and glottic airway. However, when the tracheostomy tube is occluded (capped), he develops moderate respiratory distress within 2 minutes, with audible stridor and use of accessory muscles. What is the most likely explanation for this?
options: Granuloma or tracheal stenosis at the tracheostomy stoma site is partially obstructing airflow past the capped tube
The patient has developed bilateral vocal cord palsy from GBS-related cranial nerve involvement
The patient's respiratory muscles remain too weak for the increased work of breathing through the natural airway
Tracheomalacia is causing dynamic collapse of the tracheal wall when the patient breathes through the natural airway
The stridor indicates laryngospasm triggered by anxiety about decannulation
explanation: When a tracheostomy tube is capped, the patient must breathe through the natural upper airway (around and past the tube). If a granuloma or stomal stenosis has developed at the tube site, the tube itself now acts as an obstruction — the lumen around a capped tube in a narrowed trachea is insufficient for adequate air passage. This is a common cause of failed capping trials despite a patent laryngoscopy above the tube. Bronchoscopy through the stoma (below the tube tip) is the next diagnostic step; endoscopic management or tube downsizing may be required before successful decannulation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — granuloma/stomal stenosis around the tube means the capped tube itself creates airway obstruction, even when the larynx is patent on flexible scope.
Bilateral vocal cord palsy in GBS is possible but would have been visible on the flexible laryngoscopy that confirmed a 'patent glottic airway'; this was not found.

Muscle weakness affects ventilatory endurance not typically acute-onset stridor within 2 minutes on capping; moreover, he tolerates cuff deflation well (meaning he manages some upper airway breathing).
Tracheomalacia causes expiratory flow limitation and dynamic collapse, usually appearing on forced expiration rather than causing acute inspiratory stridor within seconds of capping.
Anxiety-related laryngospasm is a diagnosis of exclusion; physical obstruction at the stoma must be ruled out first.
clinicalPearl: Stridor on tracheostomy tube capping despite a patent larynx above the tube suggests granuloma or subglottic/stomal stenosis around the tube — evaluate with bronchoscopy through the stoma.
keyClue: Immediate stridor on capping but normal flexible laryngoscopy above the level of the tube
commonExamTrap: Assuming a normal flexible laryngoscopy of the larynx rules out all obstructive pathology — stomal/subglottic stenosis below or at the tube level is not visualised by this approach.
missedConcept: Flexible laryngoscopy assesses the larynx above the tracheostomy level; it does not visualise the trachea at or below the stoma where granulomata and stenosis most commonly form.

subtopic: Splenic rupture and return to activity
clinicalStem: A 19-year-old male university student presents with a 10-day history of severe exudative pharyngitis, bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, and fatigue. He is febrile at 38.6°C. Monospot test is positive and atypical lymphocytes are seen on blood film, confirming infectious mononucleosis. He was a competitive rugby player and is keen to return to training as soon as he feels better. Splenomegaly is confirmed on ultrasound (spleen 14 cm). What is the most important advice regarding return to contact sports?
options: He must avoid all contact sports and strenuous activity for at least 3–4 weeks from symptom onset (or until splenomegaly resolves on imaging) due to risk of splenic rupture
He may return to non-contact training as soon as his fever resolves, as the spleen risk is overstated
He should avoid contact sports for 1 week only, provided he is feeling well and his lymph nodes have reduced
Splenic rupture in mononucleosis only occurs in the first 48 hours of illness; after 10 days he is safe to play
He should return to sport when his monospot becomes negative, which usually occurs within 2 weeks
explanation: Splenic rupture is the most feared acute complication of infectious mononucleosis, occurring in approximately 0.1–0.5% of cases, usually in the first 3 weeks of illness. The enlarged spleen is friable and susceptible to spontaneous or traumatic rupture; this is rapidly life-threatening. All guidelines recommend avoiding contact sports and heavy lifting for at least 3–4 weeks from symptom onset. Many specialists prefer ultrasound confirmation of splenic regression to normal size before return to contact sport in athletes. In this patient with confirmed splenomegaly on ultrasound, the risk is particularly real.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — splenic rupture risk mandates avoidance of contact sport and strenuous activity for at least 3–4 weeks; imaging confirmation of spleen regression adds further safety.
Fever resolution does not indicate that the spleen has returned to normal size; ultrasound-confirmed splenomegaly is still present.
1 week is far too short; splenomegaly and rupture risk peak in weeks 2–3 of illness.
Splenic rupture can occur throughout the first 3 weeks of illness, not just the first 48 hours; 10 days in is actually the peak risk window.
Monospot reversion and symptom resolution do not correlate reliably with normalisation of splenic size; imaging is the appropriate endpoint.
clinicalPearl: Infectious mononucleosis with confirmed splenomegaly requires at least 3–4 weeks away from contact sport; splenic rupture risk peaks in weeks 2–3 and is rapidly fatal.
keyClue: Confirmed splenomegaly on ultrasound in a competitive athlete with acute EBV mononucleosis
commonExamTrap: Clearing the patient for sport once fever resolves or monospot becomes negative — neither correlates with splenic regression, which must be confirmed clinically or on imaging.
missedConcept: The spleen in EBV mononucleosis is friable and enlarged for weeks; splenic rupture is a life-threatening complication that peaks in weeks 2–3 of illness, not at onset.
